hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: W. E. B. Du Bois seated with his wife Nina and their young daughter Yolande in a formal family portrait, circa 1901
inline_images.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois with his wife Nina and daughter Yolande, photographed around 1901, the family whose firstborn son Burghardt's death Du Bois mourns in this chapter.
inline_images.repository: New York Public Library Digital Collections

related_chapters.subject: Abernathy family
related_chapters.why: Du Bois grieves a child lost too soon; the Abernathy children describe their father's inconsolable grief and the void left by a death in the family.

related_chapters.subject: Bill Russell
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Widower Raises His Two Boys
related_chapters.why: The essay centers a father's tender devotion to his child; Russell recounts his widowed father raising two boys alone after the loss of their mother.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.chapter_title: He Shot Him in the Back Over a Cow
related_chapters.why: Du Bois writes from inside a family's sudden bereavement; Grace Hall Miller recounts the day her husband was shot and killed, leaving her to raise their children.

related_chapters.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Death and an Early Start in School
related_chapters.why: The essay turns on a child severed from a parent by death; Burns describes a mother's death that forced her to grow up overnight as a young child.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames a household shaped by death and remaking; Terry recounts how her parents, each widowed by the flu, married and built a family of twelve.

related_chapters.subject: John Carlos
related_chapters.why: The essay weighs what a parent foregoes for a child's sake; Carlos recounts the cost his own family bore, including the Christmases his children went without.

top_voice.subject: Abernathy family
top_voice.reason: The children's account of their father's grief and the unfillable void most directly echoes the essay's portrait of a parent broken by loss within the family.

closing_image.alt: Rev. Ralph David Abernathy, head of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, c. 1970s
closing_image.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division)
closing_image.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions)
closing_image.caption: Abernathy family, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. The children's account of their father's grief and the unfillable void most directly echoes the essay's portrait of a parent broken by loss within the family.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Passing of the First-Born raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Mildred Pitts Walter, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, and Peggy Jean Connor.
